name: Isamu Noguchi and the Stone Sculpture of Early India
description:
Isamu Noguchi in India, c. 1970s. The Noguchi Museum Archives, 08014. © The Isamu Noguchi Foundation and Garden Museum, New York / Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York.
Isamu Noguchi wrote in 1949 that the site of Mahabalipuram was his “first and most authentic lesson.” Between then and his death in 1988, Noguchi visited India at least eleven times. Yet there has been little investigation into the nature of this long and deep connection, and the impact it had on the sculptor’s artistic philosophy and practice.

This talk by Kalyani Madhura Ramachandran sheds light on the sculptor’s enduring interest in the art of early India, including Sanchi, Elephanta, and Mahabalipuram, among numerous other ancient sites, and traces the centrality of the “matter of sculpture” in his approach. In looking at and alongside Noguchi, this talk expands our current understanding of the sculptor’s work and calls for material approaches to the study of sculpture from early India.

This programme at MAP is part of A Closer Look, a monthly series of research-focused talks by artists, curators and art historians focusing on visual cultures of South Asia.

name: Impressions of the Forest: Plants in Prints and Pigments
description: Explore the creative possibilities of plants in this hands-on workshop with educator Namrata Bhutoria, inspired by her year-long research as part of the Impart & Nalli Fellowship. Through eco-printing and natural pigments, participants will discover how locally foraged botanicals can be transformed into prints, dyes, and illustrations while learning about sustainable artistic practices.

name: Closing Paper Gardens: Art, Botany and Empire
description:
Before we say goodbye to the plants, join us for one last curator-led walkthrough of the exhibition Paper Gardens: Art, Botany, and Empire. 

Between the 17th and 20th centuries, the Indian subcontinent was the site of extensive botanical surveys under colonial rule. Plant collection and classification were closely tied to imperial economic, medical, and political interests, and botanical illustration played a central role in this project. Bringing together a new and historically significant collection of botanical art from this period, the exhibition draws on recent scholarship to recover the often uncredited identities and histories of the Indian artists who made them.

Shrey Maurya, curator of the exhibition and Research Director at Impart, leads this walkthrough through the layered histories embedded in these artworks, and their relevance to how we understand the natural world today.

Paper Gardens: Art, Botany and Empire, marks the continuation of a sustained, years-long collaboration between MAP and Impart — an online platform fostering greater public engagement with the art and cultural histories of South Asia.

name: Make Your Own Terrarium
description:
Imagine our world, shrunk to fit inside a bottle. In 19th century England, a chance encounter with a tenacious fern sparked an idea, that of a terrarium. Today, these self-sustaining havens encapsulate a tiny piece of the great outdoors, indoors, and are a testament to human ingenuity and nature's resilience.

This immersive experience delves into the world of terrariums and plant care. Participants get to make and take home their own terrarium.

This event is part of the closing of the exhibition,  Paper Gardens: Art, Botany and Empire.

name: Crocheting The Living Botanicals
description:
Inspired by Living Botanicals, this hands-on workshop invites participants to slow down and observe the colours, textures and changing forms of plants before translating these observations into crochet. Beginning with a guided exploration of the exhibition, participants will learn beginner-friendly crochet techniques to create cascading wisteria blossoms and intertwining vines.

Through the slow, repetitive act of stitching, the workshop explores ideas of growth, seasonality and transformation. Individual creations will come together as a collaborative installation, reflecting the exhibition's themes while celebrating collective making, close observation and the many ways craft can deepen our engagement with the natural world.

name: BTS Reading Circle
description:
From enchanted lands, curses, and boons to the realities of the modern climate crisis, this reading circle invites participants to reflect on our evolving relationship with the natural worl. We invite you to explore stories and ideas that provoke, challenge, and inspire uncovering ways of understanding environmental change while drawing on collective wisdom, resistance, and imagination to learn how to listen to the planet again.

Drawing from the themes explored in Beneath the Turning Sky the sessions engage with ecology and climate through a range of texts spanning folklore, ecofeminism, Indigenous knowledge systems, environmental history, and climate critique.

Upon registration, participants will be sent text extracts that will be discussed during the session. 

This event is in conjunction with our ongoing permanent exhibition Beneath the Turning Sky.

name: Paper Gardens: See Like a Botanist
description: Join botanist, researcher, and nature educator Dr Savinaya M. S. for a walkthrough of Paper Gardens: Art, Botany and Empire. Drawing on his expertise in plant taxonomy and forest ecology, Dr Savinaya will offer fresh perspectives on the relationships between art, environment, and the many ways landscapes hold memory, history, and life.

name: Library Open Day
description:
Join us for a guided exploration of both the exhibition and the Beneath The Turning Sky Library, where you'll discover a diverse selection of texts ranging from comics and graphic narratives to fiction, poetry, and critical theory. Together, we'll reflect on how reading can open new ways of engaging with the exhibition and its themes.

The session concludes with a shared reading and browsing experience, offering participants special access to the library. Explore the collection at your own pace, share your favourite excerpts, recommend books you'd like to see included, and contribute to the evolving vision of the Beneath The Turning Sky Library as a space for collective learning and exchange.

name: The Party Don’t Stop!
description:
We need, in every community, a group of angelic troublemakers - Bayard Rustin

The joy of being unapologetically authentic is a gift that we, the queer-transgender community, can share with the world. In this 3 hour offering, we will use arts to channel our resistance by celebrating who we are. We will dance, draw and craft words to resist efforts that seek to invisibilise us. The world should know that no matter what, we will still party! 

Let our joy and celebration be their trouble. 

This event is presented in conjunction with the exhibition warrior garments, on view at MAP until August 2, 2026.

This programme is part of LGBTQIA+ programming and is an attempt to amplify narratives from within the queer community through film, dialogue, and collective reflection.
